viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Flows freely like slightly thickened water; gravity dominates at any tilt
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Forms very small, fast-moving drops; minimal bead formation; spreads rapidly on contact
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Rarely used by Pollock; too thin for controlled pouring, bleeds into canvas fibers unpredictably

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Thick at rest; thins under brushing or pouring pressure; settles back to higher viscosity quickly
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Drips slowly, forms stubby drops with clean break; minimal thread formation
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Not favored; dries matte and chalky; lacks the fluidity Pollock needed for extended drip arcs

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Flows easily; maintains thread continuity during pouring; level self-smoothing within minutes
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Long, continuous threads; clean drop formation; moderate spreading on impact
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Not available during Pollock's active period (pre-1956); contemporary artists use it as substitute

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Very stiff at rest; requires significant force to flow; minimal self-leveling
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Does not drip naturally; forms peaks and ridges; knife or palette tool required for movement
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Not useful for drip; suitable for impasto texture building

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Stiff to moderately thick; can be thinned with solvents; does not flow without medium addition
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Heavy, slow drips if thinned; long strings; significant thread formation with solvents
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Used in early career (1940s); later replaced with faster-drying commercial paints

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Highly fluid; flows like thick syrup; long working time; very slow drying
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Long continuous strings; significant spread on canvas; sinuous trails
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Used for some works; slow drying limited layering; replaced by alkyd and commercial enamels

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Ideal pouring consistency; flows in long continuous threads from stick or hardened brush; maintains arc shape during fall
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Perfect thread formation; drops bead naturally; sinuous trails as artist moves; controlled splatter on impact
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: PRIMARY MEDIUM for drip period 1947-1950; Duco nitrocellulose lacquer enamel (DuPont) allowed Pollock the exact fluidity for his signature technique. Available at hardware stores cheaply.

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Similar to Duco enamel; slightly thicker; shear-thins well during pouring; good thread length
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Slightly heavier drops; good arc formation; moderate spreading; used extensively by Pollock
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Used alongside Duco; Benjamin Moore and other commercial house paints were cheap and accessible. White house paint used for ground layers; colored enamels for marks.

viscosity_data.flow_rate_description: Moderately thick; flows under pouring; maintains thread better than water-based at similar viscosity
viscosity_data.drip_behavior: Medium drop weight; good arc control; slightly longer drying window than Duco
viscosity_data.pollock_usability: Used in various works; thicker consistency created bolder, wider lines compared to Duco

hagen_poiseuille.description: Volumetric flow rate through a cylindrical tube (e.g., paint exiting a nozzle or hole in can)
hagen_poiseuille.note: Valid only for Newtonian fluids in laminar flow (Re < 2100). For Duco enamel at 120 cP through a 3mm hole: Q ≈ 2.7 × 10⁻⁷ m³/s

reynolds_number.description: Determines whether flow is laminar or turbulent
reynolds_number.pollock_context: Duco enamel drip from 1m height, 3mm thread: Re ≈ 12 — deeply laminar, explaining the smooth sinuous lines

terminal_velocity_drop.description: Maximum velocity a paint drop reaches in free fall through air
terminal_velocity_drop.formula: v_t = sqrt( (2 * m * g) / (ρ_air * C_D * A) )

variables.rho_air: air density ≈ 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level

terminal_velocity_drop.example: Duco enamel drop, diameter 3mm, mass ≈ 5×10⁻⁵ kg: v_t ≈ 4.2 m/s
terminal_velocity_drop.note: Pollock dripped from ~0.5-1m height; drops reached 60-85% of terminal velocity on impact

capillary_pressure.description: Pressure driving paint into canvas pores (Young-Laplace equation)

variables.theta: contact angle between paint and fiber (°)

capillary_pressure.canvas_example: Raw cotton duck, pore r ≈ 10μm, Duco enamel γ = 0.032 N/m, θ ≈ 30°: ΔP ≈ 5500 Pa

spreading_radius.description: Radius of paint pool after drop impact (energy conservation model)

variables.We: Weber number = ρ v² D / γ
variables.k: empirical constant ≈ 0.9 for paint on canvas

spreading_radius.laan_formula: r = (ρ * V * v²) / (8 * γ) — simplified for low-viscosity paints

paint_thread_velocity.description: Velocity of a continuous paint thread during Pollock-style pouring

variables.tau: viscous relaxation time = η / (ρ * g * h)

surface_tension_effects.drip_formation: Surface tension resists drop detachment from tool. Drop forms when gravity force (mg) exceeds maximum surface tension force (π * d * γ). Critical diameter: d_c = sqrt(6 * V * γ / (π * ρ * g)). For Duco enamel: d_c ≈ 4-6 mm
surface_tension_effects.thread_stability: Paint thread remains intact when Ca (capillary number) = η*v/γ < 1. For Duco at 120 cP and pour velocity 0.5 m/s: Ca ≈ 1.9 — near breakup threshold, creating the characteristic thread-to-drop transition
surface_tension_effects.spreading_on_canvas: On hydrophilic canvas, contact angle θ < 90°, so paint wets and spreads. On oil-primed canvas, θ > 90°, paint beads. Spreading coefficient S = γ_solid_air - γ_solid_liquid - γ_liquid_air
surface_tension_effects.fringe_formation: At paint puddle edges, evaporation increases surface tension locally, driving inward Marangoni flow — concentrating pigment at edges (coffee-ring effect, partially suppressed by viscosity)

raw_cotton_duck.note: Highly absorbent; Frankenthaler exploited this for stain technique

raw_linen.note: Less absorbent than cotton duck; finer weave

acrylic_gessoed_canvas.note: Gesso seals pores; paint sits on surface; Pollock used this for works needing distinct layers

oil_primed_canvas.note: Essentially sealed; paint sits on top; very long working time; historical standard

hardboard_masonite.note: Non-absorbent; paint pools on surface; impasto builds up
